To solve this problem, we will first determine the surface area of the given parametric surface and then use that to find the values of $ a $ and $ b $ that satisfy the surface area constraint of 1000 square units. After that, we will compute the total electrical conductivity across the surface using the provided function.

---

### **Step 1: Surface Area Calculation**

The given parametric surface is defined by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(u, v) &= a \sin(u) \cos(v) \\
y(u, v) &= a \sin(u) \sin(v) \\
z(u, v) &= b \cos(u)
\end{aligned}
$$

with $ 0 \leq u \leq \pi $ and $ 0 \leq v < 2\pi $.

We calculate the surface area $ A $ of this surface using the formula for the surface area of a parametric surface:

$$
A = \iint_{D} \left\| \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial u} \times \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial v} \right\| \, du \, dv
$$

First, compute the partial derivatives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial u} &= \left( a \cos(u) \cos(v), a \cos(u) \sin(v), -b \sin(u) \right) \\
\frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial v} &= \left( -a \sin(u) \sin(v), a \sin(u) \cos(v), 0 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the cross product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial u} \times \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial v} &= 
\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\

-a \sin(u) \sin(v) & a \sin(u) \cos(v) & 0 \\
\end{vmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$
